quality.rationale: Requires Lorentz transformation and relativistic velocity addition to derive round-trip time and causality violation condition.

origin.question: Problem 1.6. Tachyons are hypothetical particles whose velocity is faster than light. Suppose that a tachyon transmitter emits particles of a constantvelocity $u > c$ in its rest frame. If a tachyonic message is sent to an observer at rest at a distance $L$ , how much time will elapse before a tachyonic reply can be received? How much time will elapse if the distant observer is moving directly away at velocity $v$ , and is at a distance $L$ at the instant he receives the message and replies? (Show that for $u > [1 + (1 - v^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}] / v$ the reply can be received before the signal is sent!)
origin.answer: Stationary case: $t_{\text{round trip}} = 2L / u$. Moving case: $t_{\text{total}} = L[1 / u + (1 - uv) / (u - v)]$. Condition for receiving reply before sending: $u > [1 + (1 - v^2)^{1/2}] / v$ (which implies $t_{\text{total}} < 0$).
origin.solution: Solution 1.6. When the two observers are at rest in the same Lorentzframe, we have (distance) = (velocity) × (time) and $t_{\text{round trip}} = 2L / u$ .Things are more complicated when one is moving. The moving observer's coordinates are$$t^{\prime} = \gamma (t - v x)$$$$\mathbf {x}^{\prime} = \gamma (\mathbf {x} - \mathbf {v t}),$$and the tachyon emitted from the moving frame back towards the stationary observer has velocity$$\mathrm {d} x^{\prime} / \mathrm {d} t^{\prime} = - u.$$Now $\mathrm{dx}^{\prime} = \gamma (\mathrm{dx} - \mathrm{vdt})$ , $\mathrm{dt}^{\prime} = \gamma (\mathrm{dt} - \mathrm{vdx})$ , so substituting and solving for the apparent velocity in the stationary frame gives$$- \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} / \mathrm {d} t = (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {v}) / (1 - \mathbf {u v}).$$Finally we add up times in the stationary frame $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{out}} + t_{\text{back}} =$$\mathbf{L} / \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{L}(1 - \mathbf{uv}) / (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {v}) = \mathbf{L}[1 / \mathbf {u} + (1 - \mathbf {uv}) / (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {v})]$ . One can easily calculatethat$$t_{t o t a l} < 0 \quad i f \quad u > \left[ 1 + \left(1 - v^{2}\right)^{\frac {1}{2}}\right] / v.$$A spacetime diagram is helpful in understanding this effect.![](images/082a1dbb4809a9cfc0cb00f96a9635ef222b92238eefd5c41a2f0a17f340b475.jpg)
